title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an astronaut called Chloe Banks talking about her time living on the International Space Station (ISS). For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an astronaut called Chloe Banks talking about her time living on the International Space Station (ISS). For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Chloe Banks reflects on the daily routine and psychological effects of living in microgravity.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. My name is Chloe Banks, and I’ve spent a total of six months aboard the International Space Station. Living in space is a dream come true, but it's also a constant lesson in adaptation. One of the first things you have to get used to is the lack of a 'up' or 'down'. [7]I found that the most confusing part of the first week was losing my sense of direction[/7] inside the station. You can be working on a panel that feels like a wall, only to realize it's actually the ceiling. Our schedule is managed down to the minute by ground control. [8]We are required to spend at least two hours a day on the treadmill or the stationary bike[/8], to prevent our muscles from wasting away in microgravity. It’s hard work, but essential. Food is another challenge. [9]I was surprised to find that I developed a strong craving for spicy food while in orbit[/9]. Apparently, the lack of gravity causes fluids to move to your head, which dulls your sense of taste. As for the view, it never gets old. [10]Seeing the thin blue line of the atmosphere against the blackness of space was deeply humbling[/10]. It makes the Earth look so fragile. Living in such a confined space with the same five people can be tough, but [11]having a weekly 'movie night' helped us maintain a sense of community[/11]. It was our chance to relax. The most difficult task I performed was a spacewalk to repair a solar array. [12]The feeling of isolation I felt while outside the station was truly overwhelming[/12]. It's just you and the void. Despite the danger, I wouldn't trade the experience for anything. [13]I believe that international cooperation is the most important legacy of the ISS[/13]. We all work together regardless of where we come from. As the mission commander said, [14]we are the 'advance guard' for future human exploration of Mars[/14]. It’s a privilege to be part of that journey.

dialogue.speaker: chloe
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. My name is Chloe Banks, and I’ve spent a total of six months aboard the International Space Station. Living in space is a dream come true, but it's also a constant lesson in adaptation. One of the first things you have to get used to is the lack of a 'up' or 'down'. I found that the most confusing part of the first week was losing my sense of direction inside the station. You can be working on a panel that feels like a wall, only to realize it's actually the ceiling. Our schedule is managed down to the minute by ground control. We are required to spend at least two hours a day on the treadmill or the stationary bike, to prevent our muscles from wasting away in microgravity. It’s hard work, but essential. Food is another challenge. I was surprised to find that I developed a strong craving for spicy food while in orbit. Apparently, the lack of gravity causes fluids to move to your head, which dulls your sense of taste. As for the view, it never gets old. Seeing the thin blue line of the atmosphere against the blackness of space was deeply humbling. It makes the Earth look so fragile. Living in such a confined space with the same five people can be tough, but having a weekly 'movie night' helped us maintain a sense of community. It was our chance to relax. The most difficult task I performed was a spacewalk to repair a solar array. The feeling of isolation I felt while outside the station was truly overwhelming. It's just you and the void. Despite the danger, I wouldn't trade the experience for anything. I believe that international cooperation is the most important legacy of the ISS. We all work together regardless of where we come from. As the mission commander said, we are the 'advance guard' for future human exploration of Mars. It’s a privilege to be part of that journey.

questions.question: Chloe admits that she struggled with her (7) during her first few days on the ISS.
questions.explanation: She mentions that losing her sense of direction was the most confusing part.

questions.question: Astronauts must use the (8) every day to maintain their physical health.
questions.explanation: She says they must spend two hours on the treadmill or stationary bike.

questions.question: Chloe was caught off guard by her sudden desire for (9) during the mission.
questions.explanation: She developed a strong craving for spicy food because her sense of taste was dulled.

questions.question: Chloe uses the word (10) to describe the feeling she had when looking at the Earth's atmosphere.
questions.explanation: Seeing the atmosphere was 'deeply humbling' for her.

questions.question: The crew organized a (11) as a way to bond and relax together.
questions.explanation: The weekly movie night helped maintain a sense of community.

questions.question: Chloe found the sense of (12) while working outside the station to be very intense.
questions.explanation: She describes the feeling of isolation during the spacewalk as overwhelming.

questions.question: According to Chloe, (13) is the most significant achievement of the ISS project.
questions.explanation: She believes international cooperation is the most important legacy.

questions.question: The mission commander referred to the crew as the (14) for the next phase of space travel.
questions.explanation: He called them the 'advance guard' for the exploration of Mars.
